items.text: Buffett: what matters isn't how big your circle of knowledge is, but knowing exactly where its edge lies. A small, well-understood patch beats a big fuzzy one. Most people don't lose money because they know too little — they lose because they don't realise they've wandered past what they actually understand. The most expensive phrase in investing: 'I basically get it.'

For 30 years Buffett refused to buy Microsoft — even though Bill Gates was a close friend who explained it to him personally. Friendship didn't make it understandable to him. When he finally bought Apple in 2016, he treated it not as a tech company but as a consumer brand people won't switch away from — a lens he already trusted. Your circle grows by honestly reframing, not by forcing yourself in.

You widen your circle through real work, not calendar time: reading a company's actual filings across good years and bad, comparing several rivals in the same industry, talking to people who use or sell the product, and watching how the business behaves in a downturn. Skimming headlines for ten years teaches nothing. And the harder skill is saying no to exciting things just outside your circle.
Per Buffett, what matters most about your circle of competence?
options: Making it as large as possible
Knowing precisely where its boundary is
Focusing it on technology, where returns are
items.explanation: Boundary-knowledge beats size. Catastrophic losses come from unknowingly stepping outside the circle, not from a circle being small.

items.text: Zomato looks simple (an app you use daily) but is four businesses with different economics — food delivery, quick commerce, B2B supplies, dining. The circle-of-competence lesson?
options: Consumer familiarity with a product ≠ understanding the business
Apps are always outside everyone's circle
Use the segment with the best story
items.explanation: Using a product daily creates an illusion of understanding. Competence means knowing each segment's unit economics — not being a customer.

items.text: Which is the strongest evidence a business is INSIDE your circle?
options: You've held the stock for 3 years
You can name the CEO and product line
You can forecast roughly how it makes money in 10 years and what breaks it
items.explanation: The test is predictive: can you describe the durable economics and the failure modes? Ownership, familiarity, and borrowed conviction all fail that test.

items.text: Open Divi's Laboratories on screener.in and read the short business description. Now try to say, in ONE plain sentence, how it makes money — and one thing that could seriously hurt it. If you can't, that's not a failing; it's honest evidence the business sits outside your circle today. The whole skill is knowing which side of that line a company falls on before you ever risk money on it. (~5 min)
